title: Monaco Winners and Losers: MaraDöner's Maiden Triumph, Keisner's Nightmare, and Vandesteene's Reckoning
meta: Published: 25 May 2026 | By Lena Müller, Strategy Analyst | F1CC News
caption: Michiil MaraDöner celebrates his first F1 victory — a triumph built on perfect timing and fearless overtaking around the principality.
body:
Let's rewind to lap 32, the pit exit. The rain has returned, heavier this time, and the track is flooding. Race control throws the red flag. Every driver still running — and there are fewer than you'd expect, because Monaco has already claimed its victims — files into the pit lane. The order is frozen. When the race resumes, it will be a 15‑lap sprint to the finish, everyone on intermediate tyres, the walls of the principality glistening under the returning rain. Michiil MaraDöner is ninth on the restart grid. He has nothing to lose. Forty‑five minutes later, he crosses the finish line as a grand prix winner. That single red‑flag pause, and the chaos that preceded it, turned a chaotic Monaco afternoon into a coronation. Here is who seized their moment — and who left the principality with nothing but regret.

Winners

Michiil MaraDöner (Alpine, P1 from P9): MaraDöner's first victory was not a gift. It was earned through the kind of controlled aggression that Monaco demands and rarely rewards. On the restart, he picked off cars one by one — Hicks, then Zwart, then Verhaeren — each move executed with precision and patience. By lap 48, he was second, and when Quarter Kevu's defence finally cracked at the hairpin on lap 52, MaraDöner was through. The final laps were a masterclass in wet‑weather management: no mistakes, no heroics, just a young driver delivering the biggest result of his career. What if he had been caught in the first‑lap chaos that eliminated five cars? He wasn't. What if he had hesitated at the restart? He didn't. The alternative was a midfield finish. The reality is a maiden win and a place in Alpine's history.

Quarter Kevu (Racing Bulls, P2 from P7): Kevu did what Kevu always does: he kept his head, stayed out of trouble, and let the race come to him. He started seventh, survived the first‑lap carnage, and was perfectly positioned when the red flag flew. On the restart, he took the lead and held it for ten laps before MaraDöner's relentless pressure finally told. Second place is his best result of the season, and it moves him back into the championship conversation. The alternative — a lock‑up into Sainte‑Dévote, a brush with the barriers — never happened. That's the Kevu difference.

Philippe Verhaeren (Red Bull, P3 from P11): Verhaeren's first podium of the season was a quiet, effective performance. He started 11th, avoided the chaos, and was there at the end to collect the reward. Red Bull have been searching for consistency all year, and this result — combined with Roy's fourth place — represents their best team performance of the season. Verhaeren has often been overshadowed by his teammate's headlines; this podium is a reminder of his quality.

Jason Roy (Red Bull, P4 from P8): Roy might have hoped for more, but fourth place from eighth on the grid, on a day when championship leader Eetu Väisänen finished fifth and title rival Robin Keisner crashed out, is a significant result. His pace in the wet was competitive, and he stayed out of the walls — which, at Monaco, is half the battle. He leaves the principality having gained ground on both drivers ahead of him in the standings.

Maikel Zwart (Mercedes, P7 from P14): A clean weekend. No mechanical issues. No radio screams. No pit‑lane disasters. For Maikel Zwart, this qualified as a victory of sorts. Seventh place from 14th on the grid, in a Mercedes that has been difficult all season, is a step forward. The car is still not where it needs to be, but for the first time since China, Zwart could climb out of the cockpit without a list of complaints. Small mercies.

Losers

Robin Keisner (Audi, DNF): The championship leader arrived in Monaco with momentum. He left with a wrecked car and a significant dent in his title challenge. Keisner crashed out on lap 22, losing control at the exit of the tunnel — a corner that has claimed champions before him. The DNF, combined with Väisänen's fifth place, tightens the championship at the top. Keisner still leads, but his margin has been slashed. What if he had taken a safer line, settled for points? That's not the driver he is. But Monaco does not forgive ambition.

W. Vandesteene (Mercedes, DNF + penalty): Vandesteene's race was a disaster from the first lap. A collision with Jack TEF at the hairpin earned him a drive‑through penalty and two more penalty points on his licence. He served the penalty, struggled at the back, and eventually retired. The Mercedes driver, who had shown such promise with a fourth place in Canada, reverted to the erratic form that has blighted his season. The stewards' verdict was clear; the damage to his reputation may take longer to repair.

Jack TEF (Williams, DNF): TEF didn't put a wheel wrong. He was the innocent victim of Vandesteene's misjudgment, and his race ended in the barriers. Williams had hoped for another strong result after Aygen's victory in Canada; instead, they leave Monaco with a double DNF — Aygen also retired — and a constructors' lead that has evaporated. TEF's frustration was palpable, and the points lost will hurt.

Zhao Lei (McLaren, DNF): Lei started on the front row. He had the pace to contend. But Monaco is cruel, and a mistake at the Swimming Pool complex ended his afternoon. The DNF halts his momentum and drops him further behind in the championship. McLaren's challenge for the constructors' crown will need to be rebuilt in Spain.

The first‑lap victims (Aygen, Springfield, Cachinho, Nathanial, Kondra): Five cars eliminated on the opening lap. Aygen, the Canada winner, was caught in the Sainte‑Dévote squeeze and never got going. Springfield's difficult season continued. Cachinho's search for a first point goes on. Monaco's first lap is always a lottery, and for these five, the ticket was a losing one. The alternative — a clean start — would have changed everything. Instead, their afternoons were over before the rain had even begun to fall.

Monaco delivered what Monaco always delivers: chaos, heartbreak, and an unlikely winner. MaraDöner's maiden victory is the story of the weekend, but the championship implications ripple far beyond the principality. Keisner's lead is shrinking. Red Bull are resurgent. And the sport's most glamorous race has, as always, left a trail of broken cars and broken dreams in its wake. On to Spain.
